metadata.title: Private Pool
metadata.description: Privacy-preserving cryptocurrency transfers powered by Zero-Knowledge Proofs

features.label: Features
features.heading:
A New Standard for
Complete Anonymity
features.zkProofsDesc: Verify transactions while completely hiding sender and recipient information using zero-knowledge proof technology.
features.untraceableDesc: No usage flow is exposed on-chain, making it impossible to trace who sent what to whom.
features.gameChainDesc: Optimized for game chains, offering anonymous transactions with fast speed and low fees.
features.stealthDesc: Protects recipient privacy by generating a new disposable address each time.

howItWorks.label: How It Works
howItWorks.heading:
Perfect Privacy
in 4 Steps
howItWorks.step1Desc: Connect your game chain wallet to Privacy Pool.
howItWorks.step2Desc: Deposit tokens to the smart contract and receive a secret note.
howItWorks.step3Desc: Mix with other users' transactions to ensure anonymity.
howItWorks.step4Desc: Complete an untraceable withdrawal to a new address.
howItWorks.securityDesc: ZK-SNARKs technology verifies transaction validity while hiding the contents.

compliance.label: Compliance
compliance.heading: Privacy for the public,
compliance.pillar1Desc: Block sender-recipient connection from public observers
compliance.pillar2Desc: Ensure sender-recipient and amount tracking for operators
compliance.pillar3Desc: Guarantee fund withdrawal when operator refuses or fails
compliance.privacyFirstDesc:
General users cannot know the parties or amounts of transactions.
Only regulators can review when necessary, maximizing privacy protection.
compliance.publicNote: All critical information is encrypted and completely protected from general users.
compliance.operatorNote: Accessible only when necessary, designed so that funds cannot be seized.
compliance.keyMessageContent: Core transaction information (sender, recipient, amount) is completely hidden from general users through Zero-Knowledge Proofs and encryption. Regulators can only decrypt this information <highlight>only when compliance verification is needed</highlight>, and even in this process, the design ensures user funds cannot be seized, maximizing privacy protection.
compliance.operatorApprovalDesc: Immediate fund release after compliance verification
compliance.opStep2Desc: Recalculate hash with depositor, recipient, amount, secret
compliance.opStep3Desc: Look up depositor and recipient NPK and check sanctions
compliance.opStep4Desc: Approve immediately if compliant, otherwise 24h timeout
compliance.securityGuaranteeNote: The operator can know the sender-recipient connection, but cannot seize funds as they don't hold the NSK (nullifier secret key). Additionally, the 24-hour timeout mechanism guarantees censorship resistance.

footer.description: Privacy-preserving cryptocurrency transfers powered by Zero-Knowledge Proofs.
footer.disclaimer: Private Pool is an experimental protocol. Illegal use is prohibited. Users are responsible for complying with the laws of their jurisdiction.

hero.label: Technical Documentation
hero.heading: Protocol Architecture
hero.description: A complete technical guide to understanding and integrating the Privacy Pool protocol — from ZK circuits to SDK integration.

overview.title: Overview
overview.intro1: Privacy Pool is a protocol that tackles privacy and regulatory compliance at the same time. Using ZK circuits, stealth addresses, and encrypted notes, it hides transaction details from public observers while selectively disclosing necessary information to regulators.
overview.intro2: This documentation covers everything you need to understand and work with Privacy Pool — from the end-to-end protocol flow and ZK circuit design to smart contracts, the sequencer, and SDK integration.
overview.intro3: The protocol is designed based on the paper <paperLink>Blockchain Privacy and Regulatory Compliance: Towards a Practical Equilibrium</paperLink>, the <eipLink>EIP-5564 Stealth Address</eipLink> standard, and the <poolsLink>Privacy Pools</poolsLink> project by 0xbow, optimized for zero-knowledge proof compatibility. ZK circuits are written in <noirLink>Aztec Noir</noirLink>, enabling client-side proving ensuring sensitive user data is processed securely without ever leaving the user's device.

stealthAddress.name: Stealth Address
stealthAddress.description: Recipient anonymization via one-time stealth addresses

encryptedNote.name: Encrypted Note
encryptedNote.description: Secure note delivery with efficient scanning

client.name: Client Layer
client.description: Browser-based frontend with integrated SDK and WASM proving

offChain.name: Off-Chain Infrastructure
offChain.description: Merkle tree management, note scanning, and operator services

onChain.name: On-Chain (EVM)
onChain.description: Smart contracts for deposits, withdrawals, and verification

concepts.title: Why Privacy Pool?
concepts.subtitle: How Privacy Pool solves blockchain privacy — built on the <paperLink>Privacy Pools paper</paperLink> and <eipLink>EIP-5564</eipLink>.

problem.title: The Transparency Problem
problem.desc: On a public blockchain, every transfer is fully visible: sender, recipient, and amount. Anyone with a block explorer can reconstruct who paid whom. This isn't a bug — it's how blockchains work. But it means financial privacy doesn't exist.
problem.question: Can we transfer tokens privately — while staying compliant with regulations?

locker.title: Hiding the Transfer
locker.desc: Instead of recording transfer details on-chain, Privacy Pool hashes them into a single value called a commitment. The hash is one-way — you can't reverse it to see the original data. On-chain, only the hash appears: observers know a deposit happened, but not what's inside.
locker.intuition: Think of it as a numbered locker in a large building. The commitment is the locked box; the Merkle tree is the building. An observer knows a box was placed, but can't see what's inside or who it's for.
locker.commitmentDesc: A <poseidonLink>Poseidon2</poseidonLink> hash of the transaction parameters (amount, secret, recipient key, depositor, block number). One-way: the hash reveals nothing about the inputs.
locker.commitmentFormula: commitment = poseidon2(secret, npk, amount, blockNumber, depositor)
locker.poseidonNote: Why Poseidon2? It's native to ZK arithmetic circuits (BN254 field), yielding ~29x faster proving than keccak256. Sponge construction with t=4, rate=3.

merkle.title: The Anonymity Set
merkle.desc: Commitments accumulate in a Merkle tree — a shared pool that grows with every deposit. The larger the pool, the harder it is to guess which commitment belongs to whom. At withdrawal, a user proves their commitment is somewhere in the tree — without revealing where.
merkle.termDesc: A binary hash tree that produces a single root from all its leaves. Any leaf's membership can be proven with just 32 sibling hashes — without revealing the rest of the tree.
merkle.specs: Depth: 32 | Max deposits: 2³² (~4.3B) | Proof: 32 hashes | Insert: O(log n)

keyDelivery.title: Delivering the Secret Anonymously
keyDelivery.naiveDesc: The recipient needs the secret to withdraw later. The obvious approach — encrypt it to their public key — has a fatal flaw.
keyDelivery.naiveProblem: If the recipient's public key appears on-chain, anyone can link the deposit to them. Recipient privacy is broken.
keyDelivery.solutionIntro: The fix: use a one-time throwaway key per deposit (stealth address), and encrypt with it via ECIES. On-chain, only this ephemeral key appears — no link to the real recipient.
keyDelivery.stealthTermDesc: Per <eipLink>EIP-5564</eipLink>: generate a fresh ephemeral key pair for each deposit. Only the ephemeral public key goes on-chain. No on-chain link to the recipient's real key — observers can't identify who the deposit is for.
keyDelivery.eciesTermDesc: Elliptic Curve Integrated Encryption Scheme over secp256k1. Derives a shared secret via ECDH, expands it with a KDF, then encrypts with XOR cipher + HMAC-SHA256. Used in <eipLink>EIP-5564</eipLink> for encrypted note delivery.
keyDelivery.ecdhTermDesc: Key agreement protocol: two parties compute the same shared secret from their own key pairs. No third party can derive the shared secret from public data.
keyDelivery.ecdhMathKeys:
Alice (sender):  privKey e,  pubKey E = e·G
Bob   (receiver): privKey b,  pubKey B = b·G
keyDelivery.ecdhMathKeysDesc: G is the generator point of the secp256k1 elliptic curve (public constant). Deriving a private key from a public key is computationally infeasible (discrete logarithm problem).
keyDelivery.ecdhMathShared:
Alice computes:  S = e · B = e·(b·G) = (e·b)·G
Bob computes:    S = b · E = b·(e·G) = (b·e)·G
keyDelivery.ecdhMathSharedDesc: By the associativity of elliptic curve scalar multiplication, e·(b·G) = b·(e·G). Both parties arrive at the same point S.
keyDelivery.ecdhMathSecurity: Third party knows: E = e·G, B = b·G, G  →  Computing e·b·G requires e or b, but neither can be derived (CDH problem)
keyDelivery.step1: Generate a one-time ephemeral key pair (secp256k1)
keyDelivery.step3: KDF: keccak256 counter-mode → 32B encryption key + 32B HMAC key
keyDelivery.step4: XOR stream cipher + HMAC-SHA256 integrity tag
keyDelivery.kdfDetail: KDF uses keccak256 counter mode: encKey = keccak256(shared || 0x01)[0:32], macKey = keccak256(shared || 0x02)[0:32]. Two independent 256-bit keys from one ECDH shared secret.
keyDelivery.wireFormat:
Recipient Note: [ephPubKey(33B) | ciphertext(128B) | MAC(32B) | viewTag(1B)] = 194B
Operator Note:  [ephPubKey(33B) | ciphertext(32B)  | MAC(32B)]             = 97B
keyDelivery.result: The recipient computes the same shared secret via ECDH and decrypts. Observers see only the ephemeral key — unlinkable to any identity.

noteScanning.title: Finding Your Notes
noteScanning.desc: The recipient now faces a practical problem: thousands of encrypted notes exist on-chain, and they need to find theirs. Decrypting every one is too expensive. A 1-byte view tag solves this.
noteScanning.viewTagDesc: A 1-byte filter (0–255) derived from keccak256(ECDH shared secret). Recipients compare this single byte before attempting full decryption — mismatches are skipped instantly. Inspired by Monero.
noteScanning.senderStep3: Append 1-byte viewTag to note → emit on-chain
noteScanning.receiverStep3: expected ≠ viewTag → skip immediately (99.6% of notes)
noteScanning.scanStep1: Fetch all encrypted notes from the sequencer
noteScanning.scanStep2: Compare each note's viewTag against expected — skip on mismatch
noteScanning.scanStep3: Attempt ECIES decryption only for matches (HMAC catches false positives)
noteScanning.scanStep4: Decrypted notes yield secret, amount, depositor → inputs for withdrawal
noteScanning.efficiency: 99.6% of notes are skipped without decryption. For N notes, only ~N/256 require full decryption.

checkpoint.title: Deposit Privacy: Complete
checkpoint.item1: Transfer details hidden in a Poseidon2 commitment
checkpoint.item2: Secret delivered via ECIES — no on-chain link to recipient
checkpoint.item4: Commitment mixed into the anonymity set (Merkle tree)
checkpoint.item5: Recipient finds their notes via view tag (99.6% skip rate)

withdrawProblem.title: The Withdrawal Problem
withdrawProblem.intuition: Deposits are private — but withdrawal is where privacy usually breaks. If Bob says "I want deposit #42," anyone can look up who made that deposit. The key insight: don't specify which deposit. Instead, prove you own some deposit in the pool without saying which one.
withdrawProblem.onChainDesc: Every deposit event records the depositor (msg.sender). A withdrawal referencing a specific deposit index would expose the sender-recipient link. Zero-knowledge proofs eliminate this by proving set membership over the entire Merkle tree.
withdrawProblem.transformDesc: Instead of "withdraw deposit #N," the prover says "I know the secret behind some commitment in this tree — I won't say which." The contract verifies the math and releases funds, learning nothing about which deposit was claimed.
withdrawProblem.zkpTermDesc: A proof that convinces a verifier something is true without revealing why. The verifier learns the statement is valid — nothing about the secret data behind it.
withdrawProblem.prove1: Membership: the prover owns a commitment in the Merkle tree (which one is hidden)
withdrawProblem.prove2: Correct amount: the withdrawal amount matches what was deposited
withdrawProblem.prove3: No double-spend: this commitment hasn't been withdrawn before (via nullifier)
withdrawProblem.c5Formula: poseidon2([depositor, recipient, amount, secret, DOMAIN]) == compliance_hash
withdrawProblem.nullifierDesc: A unique tag derived from the commitment's secret and the owner's key. The contract tracks used nullifiers to prevent double-withdrawal. Crucially, a nullifier can't be traced back to its source commitment.
withdrawProblem.provingTime: Proving: ~0.18s desktop, ~0.5s mobile (UltraHonk, bb.js WASM, Aztec Noir)

compliance.title: Compliance Without Surveillance
compliance.dilemma: Full anonymity creates a regulatory problem: it shields illegal activity just as well as legitimate transactions. Regulators won't accept a system where no one can verify compliance.
compliance.solutionDesc: Following the <paperLink>Privacy Pools paper</paperLink>, the protocol introduces selective disclosure: a trusted Operator can verify transactions, but the public cannot. Based on the <poolsLink>0xbow Privacy Pools</poolsLink> design, optimized for ZK proofs.
compliance.hashDesc: A hash generated at deposit time that binds depositor, recipient, amount, and a random secret. The secret prevents brute-force reversal — observers can't guess the inputs.
compliance.hashFormula: compliance_hash = poseidon2(depositor, recipient, amount, secret, DOMAIN_COMPLIANCE)
compliance.operatorStep1: Deposit: sender encrypts secret + leafIndex to the Operator's key → stored on-chain as Operator Note
compliance.operatorStep2: Withdrawal: recipient submits ZK proof (includes compliance_hash)
compliance.operatorStep3: Verification: Operator decrypts note → recovers secret → recomputes compliance_hash → checks match → runs sanctions check
compliance.operatorKnows: The Operator can link deposits to withdrawals — knowing sender, recipient, and amount. This is by design. However, the Operator cannot move funds: they lack the user's nullifier secret key (nsk), so they can't forge proofs or create valid nullifiers.
compliance.accessObserverCannot: Who sent to whom, how much
compliance.accessOperatorCannot: User's nsk — can't forge proofs or seize funds
compliance.accessContractCannot: Which deposit a withdrawal corresponds to
compliance.threatDefense1: Circuit uses original depositor from commitment → proof fails
compliance.threatDefense3: Circuit binds actual amount → proof fails
compliance.trustAssumption: The Operator knows the secret but not the user's nsk. Without nsk, they can't derive keys, generate nullifiers, or move funds. The 24h timeout fallback guarantees censorship resistance.

summary.title: The Complete Picture
summary.depositDesc: Hash transfer details into commitment → insert into Merkle tree → deliver secret via ECIES → hide recipient with stealth address
summary.withdrawDesc: Find notes via view tag → decrypt → generate ZK proof → Operator checks compliance → receive funds
summary.observerConclusion: Public observers see: "someone deposited, someone withdrew" — nothing more.
summary.operatorConclusion: Operator sees: sender, recipient, amount — for compliance only.

references.title: References
references.paper: <paperLink>Buterin, V., Illum, J., Nadler, M., Schär, F., Soleimani, A. (2023). "Blockchain Privacy and Regulatory Compliance: Towards a Practical Equilibrium."</paperLink>
references.eip5564: <eipLink>EIP-5564: Stealth Addresses</eipLink> — One-time address generation standard for recipient anonymization
references.eip191: <eip191Link>EIP-191: Signed Data Standard</eip191Link> — Signed message standard for key derivation
references.poseidon2: <poseidonLink>Grassi, L. et al. (2023). "Poseidon2: A Faster Version of the Poseidon Hash Function."</poseidonLink>

protocol.title: Protocol Flow
protocol.subtitle: Complete transfer sequence from key derivation to fund withdrawal
protocol.intro: A Privacy Pool transfer follows three phases: Setup → Transfer → Claim. Seven participants are involved — sender, recipient, sequencer, operator, smart contract, ZK circuit, and external observer. Each phase executes EIP-191 key derivation, Poseidon2 commitment, ECIES encryption, Merkle tree update, ZK proof generation, and 2-stage withdrawal in sequence.

alice.name: Sender
alice.description: Deposits tokens and creates encrypted notes for the recipient

bob.name: Recipient
bob.description: Scans notes, generates ZK proofs, and withdraws funds

observer.name: External Observer
observer.description: Can see transactions exist but cannot link sender to recipient

operator.name: Operator
operator.description: Verifies compliance by decrypting operator notes and approving withdrawals

sequencer.name: Sequencer
sequencer.description: Manages Merkle tree, indexes encrypted notes, and relays roots on-chain

pool.name: PrivacyPoolV2
pool.description: Holds deposited funds and enforces 2-stage withdrawal with ZK verification

circuit.name: ZK Circuit
circuit.description: Verifies 6 constraints including Merkle inclusion, nullifier, and compliance hash

setup.title: Setup
setup.description: Both parties derive keys from wallet signatures. The recipient shares their payment link (npk + encPubKey) with the sender.

transfer.title: Transfer
transfer.description: The sender deposits tokens with a Poseidon2 commitment and ECIES-encrypted notes. Sequencer indexes and updates the Merkle tree.

claim.title: Claim
claim.description: The recipient scans notes, generates a ZK proof, and initiates withdrawal. Operator approves or 24h timeout auto-executes.

1.title: Key Derivation
1.description: EIP-191 signature → deterministic key pair (nsk, npk, encPubKey)
1.detail: The wallet signs the message "StealthTx Key Derivation v1" via personal_sign, then generates a seed from the keccak256 hash. From the seed, the nullifier secret key (nsk), nullifier public key (npk), and encryption public key (encPubKey) are derived. The same wallet always produces the same keys, so no separate backup is needed.

2.title: Payment Link
2.description: Share public keys only (npk + encPubKey) via URL/QR
2.detail: The recipient generates a URL or QR code containing npk and encPubKey. Since only public keys are included, fund theft is impossible even if the link is exposed. The sender extracts the recipient's public keys from this link to create encrypted notes.
2.privacy: No secret keys in the link

3.title: Deposit
3.description: Poseidon2 commitment + ECIES encrypted note → on-chain deposit
3.detail: Computes a Poseidon2 commitment from amount, secret, npk, depositor, and blockNumber. Creates both a Recipient Note (ECIES-encrypted) and an Operator Note, then calls the PrivacyPoolV2 contract's deposit() function.

4.title: Indexing
4.description: Encrypted notes stored, Merkle tree updated, root submitted on-chain
4.detail: The sequencer detects on-chain Deposit events and stores encrypted notes. It inserts commitments into the Incremental Merkle Tree in O(log n), then submits a new root on-chain when the batch condition is met (100 deposits accumulated or 30 seconds elapsed).

5.title: Note Scanning
5.description: View tag filter (99.6% skip) → ECIES local decryption → OwnedNote[]
5.detail: The recipient client fetches encrypted notes from the sequencer. A 1-byte view tag comparison quickly skips 99.6% of irrelevant notes. Only matching notes are ECIES-decrypted to recover amount, secret, and senderPublicKey.
5.privacy: Private key never leaves the browser

6.title: ZK Proof
6.description: UltraHonk proof generation with 6 constraints verification
6.detail: Fetches the Merkle proof from the sequencer and computes the nullifier and compliance hash locally. Generates a witness via noir_js, then produces the proof using the UltraHonk backend (bb.js WASM). Completed client-side in ~0.18s on desktop and ~0.5s on mobile.
6.privacy: Proof reveals nothing about the sender

7.title: 2-Stage Withdrawal
7.detail: In stage 1, the ZK proof and compliance hash are submitted on-chain to initiate withdrawal (initiateWithdrawal). In stage 2, the operator decrypts the Operator Note for KYC verification and approves immediately (attestWithdrawal), or after a 24-hour timeout anyone can call claimWithdrawal to receive the funds.

operatorNote.title: Operator Note
operatorNote.onChainDesc: The depositor (msg.sender at deposit), amount, and recipient (revealed at withdrawal) are each recorded on-chain and visible to anyone — but no one can tell which deposit corresponds to which withdrawal. Only the operator can recover the secret from the Operator Note and recalculate the compliance hash with depositor + recipient + amount + secret, verifying the link between a specific deposit and withdrawal.

circuit.title: ZK Circuit
circuit.subtitle: Poseidon2-based zero-knowledge circuit with 6 constraints
circuit.intro: The ZK circuit proves "I am authorized to withdraw these funds" without ever revealing secret keys, transaction amounts, or sender-recipient identities. Six constraints verify key ownership, deposit existence, double-spend prevention, amount range, regulatory binding, and KYC registration in a single proof — the verifier only sees true/false, with no access to the underlying data. Using Poseidon2 Sponge (t=4, rate=3) as its hash function achieves 29x faster proving time and 51x less peak memory compared to keccak256.
circuit.c5Formula: poseidon2([depositor, recipient, amount, secret, DOMAIN]) == compliance_hash
circuit.poseidonDesc: Sponge is a hash construction that absorbs inputs into a fixed-size state and then squeezes out the result. t=4 means the internal state holds 4 field elements, and rate=3 means 3 are absorbed at a time. Built on finite field arithmetic, it operates natively in ZK circuits, producing dramatic performance gains over bit-operation-based keccak256.
circuit.domainDesc: When the same Poseidon2 hash function is used for multiple purposes (commitment, nullifier, Merkle node, etc.), a unique constant is appended to each input to prevent accidental hash collisions across different usages.

contracts.title: Smart Contracts
contracts.subtitle: PrivacyPoolV2 — Off-chain Poseidon2 Merkle, 2-stage withdrawal
contracts.intro: The PrivacyPoolV2 contract handles fund custody, Merkle root management, ZK proof verification, and 2-stage withdrawal. Merkle roots use a Dual-Approval structure where the Relayer proposes and the Operator confirms after independent calculation, preventing single-point manipulation. Withdrawals offer two paths — immediate operator approval or 24-hour timeout auto-execution — ensuring censorship resistance.
contracts.roleOperatorConfirm: Independent calculation then root confirmation (Dual-approval Stage 2)
contracts.dualApprovalDesc: Merkle roots require two independent parties (Relayer + Operator) to prevent single point of manipulation. Relayer proposes, Operator confirms after independent calculation.
contracts.testsNote: 73 Foundry tests covering dual-approval, registration, and 2-stage withdrawal

sequencer.title: Sequencer
sequencer.subtitle: Unified off-chain service — Incremental Merkle Tree + Note Scanner + Operator
sequencer.intro: The sequencer unifies three roles into a single off-chain service: Tree Manager, Note Scanner, and Operator Service. It supports O(log n) insertion and proof via an Incremental Merkle Tree, collects encrypted notes and serves raw data for client scanning, and handles compliance decryption and KYC registration. Batches are triggered at 100 deposits or after 30 seconds.

treeManager.title: Tree Manager
treeManager.description: Incremental Merkle Tree — O(log n) insert/proof

merkle.title: Incremental Merkle Tree
merkle.description: Replaces full tree rebuild O(n·log n) with O(log n) insertions. Stores only non-default nodes using Map. Zero hash chain pre-computed for depth 32.

api.title: HTTP API Endpoints
api.root: Current root + leaf count + last processed index
api.registrationRoot: Registration Tree current root + leaf count

sdk.title: SDK
sdk.subtitle: Browser ZK proving + ethers.js integration TypeScript library
sdk.intro: The TypeScript SDK consists of 9 modules: crypto, keys, merkle, notes, witness, prover, wallet, contracts, and sequencer. It derives keys from EIP-1193 signatures, skips 99.6% of irrelevant notes via view tag filtering before ECIES decryption, and generates UltraHonk proofs in the browser using bb.js WASM. The Client API exposes 8 methods covering the full flow from init to claimWithdrawal.

keyDerivation.title: Key Derivation Flow
keyDerivation.note: Same wallet = same keys (no backup needed). Cached in localStorage.

compliance.title: Compliance
compliance.subtitle: How Privacy Pool satisfies privacy, regulatory compliance, and censorship resistance simultaneously
compliance.intro: Privacy Pool satisfies three requirements simultaneously: privacy (ZK proof + stealth address), regulatory compliance (compliance hash + ECIES), and censorship resistance (24h timeout fallback). The compliance hash binds depositor, recipient, amount, and secret via Poseidon2 with ZK circuit integrity enforcement. The 2-stage withdrawal and KYC Registration Tree balance operator verification with user protection.

privacy.title: Privacy
privacy.description: Block sender-recipient connection from public observers

regulatory.title: Regulatory Compliance
regulatory.description: Ensure sender-recipient and amount tracking for operators

censorship.title: Censorship Resistance
censorship.description: Guarantee fund withdrawal when operator refuses or fails

complianceHash.title: Compliance Hash Binding
complianceHash.formula: compliance_hash = poseidon2(depositor, recipient, amount, secret, DOMAIN_COMPLIANCE)
complianceHash.description: ZK circuit constraint 5 guarantees integrity. Any tampering with depositor, recipient, or amount causes proof failure.

row1.defense: Circuit uses original depositor from commitment → proof failure

row2.defense: Circuit uses public input recipient → on-chain mismatch

row3.defense: Circuit uses actual transfer_amount → proof failure

row4.defense: Secret salt → observer doesn't know secret → infeasible

stage1.label: Stage 1: Initiate
stage1.description: ZK proof verification + compliance hash on-chain recording + pending status

stage2a.label: Stage 2a: Operator Approval
stage2a.description: Immediate fund release after compliance verification

stage2b.label: Stage 2b: 24h Timeout
stage2b.description: Auto-execution if operator doesn't respond (censorship resistant)

kycTree.title: KYC Registration Tree
kycTree.description: Before withdrawing funds, users must complete KYC verification and submit their NPK to the operator. The operator registers the verified NPK in the Registration Tree (identity tree), recording the user as a verified participant. During withdrawal, ZK circuit constraint 6 checks whether the user's NPK is included in the identity tree — unregistered users cannot generate a valid proof and are blocked from withdrawing. This ensures that only verified users can participate in the protocol while preserving their privacy.

verification.title: Operator Verification Process
verification.step1: Decrypt Operator Note — ECIES decryption → recover secret
verification.step2: Verify Compliance Hash — Recalculate hash with depositor, recipient, amount, secret
verification.step3: KYC Check — Look up depositor and recipient NPK, check sanctions
verification.step4: Policy Decision — Approve if compliant, otherwise 24h timeout auto-execution

compliance.securityNote: The operator can know the sender-recipient connection but cannot seize funds (no NSK). The 24h timeout guarantees censorship resistance.
